title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You have read a report stating that young people in your city are struggling to find affordable housing. Write a letter to your local council explaining why more affordable housing is urgently needed for young people, describing the problems caused by the current shortage, and suggesting practical solutions the council could implement.

Write your letter to the council.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Council Members,

I am writing to express my deep concern regarding the severe shortage of affordable housing for young people in our city. This issue is having a significant and damaging impact on many residents, and I urge the council to take immediate action.

At present, a large number of young adults are forced to remain in the family home well into their twenties due to unaffordable rents and house prices. Those who do manage to rent independently are frequently spending over fifty percent of their income on accommodation alone, leaving very little for other essentials. This creates considerable financial stress and limits young people's ability to pursue education or career opportunities.

I would strongly recommend that the council consider several practical measures: increasing the supply of social housing, introducing rent caps for private landlords, and offering subsidised shared housing schemes specifically targeted at people under thirty.

I sincerely hope the council will treat this matter with the urgency it deserves.

Yours faithfully,
A Concerned Resident

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'The Boomerang Generation: Why are so many young adults moving back in with their parents — and is it really such a bad thing? We want articles from young people sharing their experiences and opinions.'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Home Sweet Home — Or Is It?

In recent years, a growing number of young adults have found themselves doing something their parents never imagined: moving back in with the family. This so-called 'boomerang generation' is a product of soaring rents, stagnant wages, and increasingly competitive job markets.

For many, returning home is a practical necessity rather than a lifestyle choice. Living with parents allows young people to save money, pay off student debts, and build a financial foundation that would otherwise take years to achieve independently. In many cultures, moreover, multi-generational living has always been perfectly normal and even desirable.

That said, it is not without challenges. Privacy, independence, and the sense of adult identity can all suffer when you are once again sharing a fridge with your parents.

Ultimately, there is no shame in returning home if it is the smartest financial decision available. What matters is having a clear plan, setting boundaries, and using the time wisely to build towards genuine independence.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt:
Your college has asked you to write a report on the results of a survey about students' housing preferences and living arrangements. Include findings about where students currently live, what type of housing they would prefer, and recommendations for how the college could better support students with housing needs.

Write your report.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Student Housing Preferences at Westfield College

Introduction
This report summarises the findings of a recent survey completed by 210 students regarding their current housing situations and future preferences.

Current Living Arrangements
The survey revealed that 48% of students currently live with their parents, 31% rent privately in shared accommodation, and only 11% reside in college-managed housing. A significant proportion of those renting privately reported spending more than 40% of their monthly income on rent, which many described as financially unsustainable.

Housing Preferences
When asked about their ideal living arrangement, the majority of respondents expressed a preference for affordable, college-managed accommodation close to campus. Many students cited safety, affordability, and proximity to academic facilities as their primary concerns.

Recommendations
Based on these findings, the college should consider expanding its managed housing stock, negotiating partnerships with local private landlords to secure discounted rates for students, and creating a dedicated housing advice service. Additionally, a peer support network connecting students seeking flatmates would be highly beneficial.

Conclusion
Addressing students' housing concerns would directly improve academic performance and overall wellbeing.
